message.content:
You have access to a bash tool (mcp__workspace__bash) and to Vercel MCP tools under the server prefix mcp__bb8e289f-e6ae-47d8-a130-62b8ccb4bf8d__ (these may be deferred — use ToolSearch with query "select:mcp__bb8e289f-e6ae-47d8-a130-62b8ccb4bf8d__deploy_to_vercel,mcp__bb8e289f-e6ae-47d8-a130-62b8ccb4bf8d__list_projects,mcp__bb8e289f-e6ae-47d8-a130-62b8ccb4bf8d__get_deployment,mcp__bb8e289f-e6ae-47d8-a130-62b8ccb4bf8d__list_teams" to load them first).

GOAL: Deploy a complete, ready-to-go Next.js 15 (App Router, Tailwind CSS v4) project to the user's Vercel account via the deploy_to_vercel tool, using a direct file-tree deploy (no git needed).

All project files already exist on disk at: /sessions/tender-brave-bell/mnt/outputs/deploy-project/
File list (14 files, already verified complete and correct — do not modify their content, just read and upload them):
- package.json
- next.config.mjs
- postcss.config.mjs
- tsconfig.json
- app/layout.tsx
- app/globals.css
- app/page.tsx
- app/Navbar.jsx
- app/Experience.jsx
- app/Projects.jsx
- app/FloatingActions.jsx
- app/hats/page.tsx
- public/mypic.jpg (58KB JPEG, a real photo — binary file)
- public/bee.gif (56KB animated GIF — binary file)

STEPS:
1. Read each text file's content via bash (`cat`), and base64-encode the two binary files (public/mypic.jpg and public/bee.gif) via bash (`base64 -w0 <file>`). These are small (~80KB base64 each) so should be fine to read directly.
2. Call the Vercel deploy tool (likely named deploy_to_vercel) with:
   - teamId: "team_NFecgrPKTeIDY2vihW7kXCVa" (team name "Senjuti's projects")
   - project name: "senjuti-bala-portfolio" (or similar valid slug; check list_projects first in case a project with a similar name already exists, and reuse/update it if so)
   - The full file tree: all 12 text files with their exact content and correct relative paths (e.g. "app/page.tsx", "public/mypic.jpg"), and the 2 binary files with `encoding: "base64"` and the base64 content you generated.
   - target: production (this is a real deploy the user explicitly asked for — they said "okay" to proceeding with deployment to their Vercel account).
3. Wait for/check the deployment status (use get_deployment or list_deployments if needed) until it's ready, and note the resulting deployment URL (e.g. something like senjuti-bala-portfolio.vercel.app).
4. If the deploy fails (e.g. a build error), read the build logs (get_deployment_build_logs), diagnose the issue (common issues: Tailwind v4 config, missing dependency, TypeScript error), fix the relevant file(s) on disk at /sessions/tender-brave-bell/mnt/outputs/deploy-project/, and redeploy. Iterate up to 3 times if needed.
5. Once the deploy succeeds, attempt to attach the custom domain senjutibala.com to this project (there may be a tool for this, or it may need to be done via the Vercel dashboard — check what domain-related tools are available under the mcp__bb8e289f-e6ae-47d8-a130-62b8ccb4bf8d__ prefix, e.g. anything with "domain" in the name). Background: senjutibala.com is registered at Name.com but its nameservers already point to Vercel DNS (ns1/ns2.vercel-dns.com), so it was likely purchased through/for Vercel-DNS use previously — it should attach without needing nameserver changes, though it will need to be added to the project explicitly since currently zero projects exist in this Vercel account.
6. If a domain tool exists and attaching succeeds, confirm the domain is now associated with the project. If no such tool exists or it requires manual dashboard steps, note exactly what those steps are.

IMPORTANT CONSTRAINTS:
- Do not enter any payment information or purchase anything — if attaching the domain requires a purchase or payment step, stop and report that back instead.
- Do not modify the design/content of the source files beyond fixing genuine build errors (e.g. syntax typos introduced by encoding) — the content has already been finalized with the user through many rounds of review.

REPORT BACK (this is what matters most — be concise, under 300 words):
- Whether the deploy succeeded, and the live Vercel URL.
- Whether the domain senjutibala.com was successfully attached, or what manual step remains if not.
- Any build errors you had to fix, described briefly.

attachment.content:
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use only when the user explicitly asks to run, see, or set up their morning brief, or if they invoke /morning by name. A question about their day, schedule, or calendar is not by itself a request for the brief; answer it directly instead.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
===app/layout.tsx=== import type { Metadata } from "next"; import "./globals.css"; export const metadata: Metadata = { title: "Senjuti Bala", description: "Data scientist working across agent-based modelling, simulation, FAIR data, and visualization.", }; export default function RootLayout({ children, }: Readonly<{ children: React.ReactNode; }>) { return ( {children} ); } ===app/globals.css=== @import "tailwindcss"; :root { --bg: #FFFFFF; --ink: #15140F; --muted: #6B6A62; --faint: #9B998F; --border: rgba(21, 20, 15, 0.14); --border-soft: rgba(21, 20, 15, 0.08); --accent: #4169E1; --accent-soft: #E7EBFB; --font-sans: 'Google Sans', sans-serif; --font-voice: 'Google Sans', sans-serif; } * { box-sizing: border-box; } ::selection { background-color: var(--ink); color: var(--bg); } /* Accordion body height animation, driven from a --xp-h custom property set inline per item */ .xp-body { max-height: 0; overflow: hidden; transition: max-height 0.25s ease; } .xp-body[data-open="true"] { max-height: var(--xp-h, 600px); } ===app/page.tsx=== import Image from "next/image" import Navbar from "./Navbar" import Experience from "./Experience" import Projects from "./Projects" import FloatingActions from "./FloatingActions" export default function Home() { return ( <>
{/* Hero / profile summary, doubles as About */}

Senjuti Bala

{/* Photo floats beside the first paragraph only */}

I am a data scientist with roots in research, consulting, and software development. I enjoy working at the intersection of data, systems, and behavioral science. I trained as an engineer and spent three years doing technical consulting for the telecommunications sector in Bangladesh. I am most at home figuring out how patterns actually work. Agents are my friends, and a simulation is my playground. I previously worked on pricing and product modelling as a technical consultant. I also co-authored a book chapter in{" "} Fair Data Fair Africa Fair World, where I built a knowledge graph from the testimonies of trafficking survivors. My MSc thesis introduced a two-layer agent-based model of climate negotiations among three types of countries. I am currently extending it so the agents can learn to make better choices.

I am based in Leiden, the Netherlands, and currently looking for opportunities in data and applied AI. I received my master’s degree from Leiden University in computer science. I previously studied information technology at the National Institute of Technology, Durgapur.

Skills: agent-based modelling, statistical modelling, RAG, Python (Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn, Matplotlib, Statsmodels, Plotly, Mesa), PyTorch, SQL, FAIR data, OWL/RDF ontology design, SPARQL, Power BI, and Streamlit. Fluent in English and Bengali, and a beginner in Dutch.

balasenjuti22@gmail.com LinkedIn GitHub Book a chat Instagram Spotify

ICCR Full Scholarship: a government-funded, four-year BTech. JENESYS Exchange Fellowship: a fully funded exchange to Japan.

Volunteers with Green Kitchen Leiden on food waste reduction.

Hobbies include long-distance trekking, cycling, and films.

The model tests fairness mechanisms, international aid flows, and bounded rationality across 7 scenarios over a 100-year horizon, run across 100 seeds. The key finding is that access matters more than awareness.{" "} Code is available on GitHub. ), tags: ["Python", "Mesa ABM", "Agent-Based Modelling", "Pandas"], }, { label: "Co-authored chapter, Fair Data Fair Africa Fair World, 2025", title: "FAIR Data Implementation for Analysis of Research Data in Human Trafficking and Migration", href: "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/391762371", description: ( <> I co-led the FAIRification of 126 ethnographic interviews with trafficking survivors across Libya, Sudan, Ethiopia, and Europe. I built a custom OWL ontology, converted records to RDF and Turtle using rdflib, and loaded them into AllegroGraph for SPARQL querying. I also built a Streamlit dashboard and Gephi network graphs for non-technical researchers, under full DPIA and GDPR compliance.{" "} DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.15383037 ), tags: ["FAIR Principles", "RDF/OWL", "SPARQL", "Streamlit", "Network Analysis"], }, { label: "Independent project", title: "Crop Yield Prediction for SDG2", href: "https://github.com/tijushe/crop-yield-prediction-SDG2", description: "A machine learning model that predicts crop yields from agricultural and climate data, built in support of UN Sustainable Development Goal 2, Zero Hunger.", tags: ["Python", "Scikit-learn", "Regression"], }, { label: "Independent project", title: "Bias Detection in News Sentences", href: "https://github.com/tijushe/bias-detection-news-sentences", description: "A text classification model that detects bias at the sentence level in news articles.", tags: ["Python", "NLP", "Text Classification"], }, { label: "Independent project", title: "Neural Reranking for MS MARCO", href: "https://github.com/tijushe/neural-reranking-msmarco", description: "A neural reranking model for passage retrieval on the MS MARCO dataset.", tags: ["Python", "PyTorch", "Information Retrieval", "Transformers"], }, ] export default function Projects() { return (

Open to work Download CV
) } ===app/hats/page.tsx=== import Navbar from "../Navbar" export default function Hats() { return ( <>

Off the clock

“I wear many hats.”

The rest of what I make, outside spreadsheets and simulations. Mostly a camera, and before that, a lot of fonts.

Photography

I got my first DSLR, a Canon 600D, in my early teens. I spent the next five years carrying multiple lenses, a tripod, and a camera bag everywhere, like it was a personality. It was. That same camera eventually led to two short films made with my best friend, both selected at the Children’s Film Festival in Dhaka, one of them a prize winner.

photo grid, placeholder

Graphic design

In college I ran my own small team of designers and developers. I still quietly judge people by the fonts they choose.

design samples, placeholder
Home
) }

content.text: Right, I need to use the workspace bash tool to read these since they're in the isolated Linux environment, not the Read tool. Let me cat them via bash instead.

content.content:
Error: result (78,120 characters across 1 line) exceeds maximum allowed tokens. Output has been saved to /var/folders/bm/wqh260px2150pcybs_9494f80000gn/T/claude-hostloop-plugins/9b9056a56a399848/projects/-Users-senjutibala-Library-Application-Support-Claude-local-agent-mode-sessions-86ced41b-35d7-4345-a4fe-754da88628dd-c98bdeed-421c-4609-bdf7-0955d0016eb7-local-93492b25-1c88-499c-aa9a-bfd0a22c9e4b-out-z7hwdx/029ace85-b5eb-4785-a18f-dd1ec186617c/tool-results/mcp-workspace-bash-1784634846132.txt.
Format: Plain text
- For targeted searches (find a string): use grep on the file directly.
- For analysis or summarization that requires reading the full content: the file's lines are too long for Read's offset/limit. Slice by character range via Bash instead — e.g. python3 -c "print(open('/var/folders/bm/wqh260px2150pcybs_9494f80000gn/T/claude-hostloop-plugins/9b9056a56a399848/projects/-Users-senjutibala-Library-Application-Support-Claude-local-agent-mode-sessions-86ced41b-35d7-4345-a4fe-754da88628dd-c98bdeed-421c-4609-bdf7-0955d0016eb7-local-93492b25-1c88-499c-aa9a-bfd0a22c9e4b-out-z7hwdx/029ace85-b5eb-4785-a18f-dd1ec186617c/tool-results/mcp-workspace-bash-1784634846132.txt').read()[A:B])" in ~80,000-char spans until you have read 100% of it.
- If the Agent tool is available, do this inside a subagent so the full output stays out of your main context. Give it the instruction above verbatim, and be explicit about what it must return — e.g. "Slice /var/folders/bm/wqh260px2150pcybs_9494f80000gn/T/claude-hostloop-plugins/9b9056a56a399848/projects/-Users-senjutibala-Library-Application-Support-Claude-local-agent-mode-sessions-86ced41b-35d7-4345-a4fe-754da88628dd-c98bdeed-421c-4609-bdf7-0955d0016eb7-local-93492b25-1c88-499c-aa9a-bfd0a22c9e4b-out-z7hwdx/029ace85-b5eb-4785-a18f-dd1ec186617c/tool-results/mcp-workspace-bash-1784634846132.txt in ~80,000-char spans via python (read()[A:B]) until you have read all 78,120 characters, then summarize and quote any key findings verbatim." A vague "summarize this" may lose detail.

content.text: I need a different approach since I can't get this large base64 string directly into my own context reasonably, but I actually need it for the tool call payload. Let me instead write a small script that outputs it in chunks I can concatenate, or better — split it into manageable pieces and reassemble.

input.command: cd /tmp && split -b 4000 mypic.b64 mypic_part_ && ls mypic_part_* | wc -l && split -b 4000 bee.b64 bee_part_ && ls bee_part_* | wc -l

content.text: That's a lot of parts to reassemble manually and error-prone. Let me think of a better approach — read each part with moderate size (e.g. 20 chunks of ~4000 chars is manageable but tedious). Let me try larger chunks, closer to the token limit, to minimize round trips.

input.command:
rm -f /tmp/mypic_part_* /tmp/bee_part_*
cd /tmp && split -b 20000 -d -a 2 mypic.b64 mypic_part_ && ls mypic_part_* && echo "---" && split -b 20000 -d -a 2 bee.b64 bee_part_ && ls bee_part_*

content.text:
mypic_part_00
mypic_part_01
mypic_part_02
mypic_part_03
---
bee_part_00
bee_part_01
bee_part_02
bee_part_03
4 parts each, ~20000 chars per part which should be safe under the token limit. Let me read them.








All 4 parts of mypic.jpg read successfully. Now let's read the bee.gif parts.








All 4 parts of bee.gif read successfully. Now let me verify the reassembly matches the original before uploading, then build the deploy payload.

input.command:
cd /tmp && cat mypic_part_00 mypic_part_01 mypic_part_02 mypic_part_03 > mypic_reassembled.b64 && diff mypic.b64 mypic_reassembled.b64 && echo "MYPIC MATCH"
cat bee_part_00 bee_part_01 bee_part_02 bee_part_03 > bee_reassembled.b64 && diff bee.b64 bee_reassembled.b64 && echo "BEE MATCH"

content.text:
MYPIC MATCH
BEE MATCH
Both files verified byte-for-byte. Now I'll deploy to Vercel with the full file tree.

const linkClass = "text-[var(--accent)] border-b border-[var(--border)] hover:text-[var(--ink)] hover:border-[var(--ink)] transition-colors" const projects = [ { label: "MSc thesis, Leiden University (LIACS), 2025", title: "Two-Layer Agent-Based Model for Climate Cooperation", href: "https://theses.liacs.nl/3627", description: ( <> A two-layer agent-based model that couples national climate-policy agents (developed, developing, and vulnerable country types) with consumer-level green technology adoption. The model tests fairness mechanisms, international aid flows, and bounded rationality across 7 scenarios over a 100-year horizon, run across 100 seeds. The key finding is that access matters more than awareness.{" "} Code is available on GitHub. ), tags: ["Python", "Mesa ABM", "Agent-Based Modelling", "Pandas"], }, { label: "Co-authored chapter, Fair Data Fair Africa Fair World, 2025", title: "FAIR Data Implementation for Analysis of Research Data in Human Trafficking and Migration", href: "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/391762371", description: ( <> I co-led the FAIRification of 126 ethnographic interviews with trafficking survivors across Libya, Sudan, Ethiopia, and Europe. I built a custom OWL ontology, converted records to RDF and Turtle using rdflib, and loaded them into AllegroGraph for SPARQL querying. I also built a Streamlit dashboard and Gephi network graphs for non-technical researchers, under full DPIA and GDPR compliance.{" "} DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.15383037 ), tags: ["FAIR Principles", "RDF/OWL", "SPARQL", "Streamlit", "Network Analysis"], }, { label: "Independent project", title: "Crop Yield Prediction for SDG2", href: "https://github.com/tijushe/crop-yield-prediction-SDG2", description: "A machine learning model that predicts crop yields from agricultural and climate data, built in support of UN Sustainable Development Goal 2, Zero Hunger.", tags: ["Python", "Scikit-learn", "Regression"], }, { label: "Independent project", title: "Bias Detection in News Sentences", href: "https://github.com/tijushe/bias-detection-news-sentences", description: "A text classification model that detects bias at the sentence level in news articles.", tags: ["Python", "NLP", "Text Classification"], }, { label: "Independent project", title: "Neural Reranking for MS MARCO", href: "https://github.com/tijushe/neural-reranking-msmarco", description: "A neural reranking model for passage retrieval on the MS MARCO dataset.", tags: ["Python", "PyTorch", "Information Retrieval", "Transformers"], }, ] export default function Projects() { return (

import Navbar from "../Navbar" export default function Hats() { return ( <>

Off the clock

“I wear many hats.”

The rest of what I make, outside spreadsheets and simulations. Mostly a camera, and before that, a lot of fonts.

Photography

I got my first DSLR, a Canon 600D, in my early teens. I spent the next five years carrying multiple lenses, a tripod, and a camera bag everywhere, like it was a personality. It was. That same camera eventually led to two short films made with my best friend, both selected at the Children’s Film Festival in Dhaka, one of them a prize winner.

photo grid, placeholder

Graphic design

In college I ran my own small team of designers and developers. I still quietly judge people by the fonts they choose.

design samples, placeholder
Home
) }
